comments.author: @seann8293
comments.text: Thank you for this superbly beautiful and instructive documentary. Miraculously outstanding and exceptionally blessed Africa: The Sahara, a desert the size of the entire US (yet commonly shown onto Earth’s map Africa as smaller or the size of the said US), with a sea and oasis within, unbelievably well-adapted Humans and animals, millennia-preserved arts, and not even a millionth of its natural resources mined yet (i.e., aside from petroleum, the largest underground water reservoir on Earth, gold, lithium, iron, uranium, salts, phosphates, etc.). No wonder Western-sponsored and fomented wars are unceasingly maintained there, and elsewhere all over Africa, for the greedy purpose of looting Africa’s natural (and even human) resources!

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K

comments.text: The Sahara is the earth's THIRD largest desert.  The largest is the Antarctic desert and the second largest is the Arctic desert.  The Sahara is the largest HOT desert.  Millions of humans live in the Sahara.

comments.author: @Earthcue
comments.text: The Sahara Desert is basically feeding the Amazon Rainforest! Every year, about 22,000 tons of phosphorus-rich Saharan dust blows all the way across the Atlantic Ocean and lands in the Amazon, replacing nutrients lost to flooding. NASA satellites have confirmed this! Nature is WILD! 🌍

replies.author: @Earthcue
replies.text: @frankdunbar4140 Oh wow, that's such a great point!! Those pink and orange sunsets over Florida? A lot of that gorgeous color comes straight from Saharan dust particles floating in the atmosphere. Thanks for adding this — you just made the fun fact even more mind-blowing!

replies.author: @frankdunbar4140
replies.text: Sahara dust also goes over Florida and affects the precipitation and sunrise and sunset colors.

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: Nature truly is amazing, thanks for sharing that fascinating insight! 😊

comments.author: @Aventurex-686
comments.text: The Sahara looks like Earth switched to minimalist mode and forgot to switch back. Endless dunes, blazing sun, and silence so deep it almost has its own soundtrack. Meanwhile, I start looking for shade after ten minutes outside. Nature definitely has a different definition of "a warm day."
